sentences.text: They took me out from this driveway.
With this little-- I'm holding this little soup, and here a little package with a [INAUDIBLE] is left-- where I was having for my son, a little pancakes or something for myself.
I was writing to this old lady where she was-- I rented just recently a little corner where I can pay her.
I don't know where.
And they take me-- excuse me-- they took me, and I said to myself, he takes me over-- I know, and I know who you are.
And I said, sir, what is it?
You got the wrong-- look, look, my document.
Look, my document.
Here I have a document.
I am Polish Catholic.
Let's go just-- he took me to his-- it's a little-- it's kind of a-- a precinct like you would say.
It must have been a private house there, and it's only Ukrainians.
They were cooperating with Germans, also going in uniforms, Ukrainian uniforms.
They don't like it in Ukraine when you tell them here in this country.
I saw marching in the village once, and they were-- well, anyway.
And then I say to myself, Mary, that's the end.
I wonder what's going to be there with [INAUDIBLE].
What's going to be end?
Maybe the old lady's going to raise him maybe.
I came in, and I sit, and right way I feel like going to the toilet.
You know, the nerves.
I took out the-- the ID with my picture there, from the Polish Comitet.
I said, here, here sir, here.
He gave it back to me.
He said, ma'am, there is a  Polish [NON-ENGLISH], like the one when I was standing for bread.
You remember, I told you she excluded me.
This one also recognized I'm-- in Lviv, in Lemberg.
I don't know her, but I was told later.
This man said, there is a [NON-ENGLISH], and she knows you and you are a Jew.
And she went there, and she told-- she told them, you want-- they were going after me.
They was-- where I am, and they find me, and they look for this proper place me--and they arrested me.
And there's no-- no point.
Then he says to me, where's your little son?
I said, I don't have a little son.
I have a little girl.
And he said, oh, yes?
What do you have here in your package?
And I said, a few things.
He took this package.
I'll never forget.
He opened the package.
He said, this is the little girl's things?
Those are boy's.
And I said-- and I say to myself, just give up.
There's no point.
And I'm sitting, and I said, I'm sorry, I sitting a little while-- I have to go to the bathroom.
Please.
He told them-- Ukrainian, the young guys, take her to the bathroom.
Watch her.
He took me to the bathroom, closed the door.
It's-- these-- primitive, but you can flush.
At least, after all, it was an office, kind of.
And he was there, and I say to myself-- and it was such an old, jalopy house-- and there was the window, a small window.
It was inside-- was so broken.
I put this thing, and I started to whine-- ah, ah.
I whined to make noise.
And I opened this little thing.
It's so very little I hardly could go squeeze.
I say, well, I have nothing to loose.
I squeeze myself through.
This is going to get-- I can't explain.
It's going to get in with the roof some.
I squeeze out.
I'll never forget.
Again, it was, again, a nice day.
And I was running, my blood going through my-- my face, blood-- my face, my arm.
And I was running, running, running, running-- and I say, I have nothing to lose.
And I looked, and nobody runs after me.
He thought I'm still inside.
And I run to this old lady with-- I don't know how, when, where I took a tram.
I don't know, but I came.
I said, let's go.
Let's go.
By the way, right away-- I left-- when I walked the streets of Lemberg.
I saw a friend of mine, a Polack.
Could you believe it?
Klimka?
I knew him.
He was a friend of Kola's also, but he was more a little decent-- more, he wasn't alcoholic.
And he punched me.
And he says, Klima, Klima!
He said, punch me.
Punch me.
Is this you?
Mary?
You are alive, and this.
And we talked and talked, and he told me he left Rovno because they wanted to meet the army, the Germans.
Well, anyway.
We lost touch.
He was a Gentile, what I'm saying.
Now, I run.
I run, I run.
I figure when I would know where Klimka is.
I should have his address, but he wouldn't hide me.
He wouldn't help me.
He couldn't possibly.
He would be afraid, and he doesn't care about Jews, but he was kind of a-- kind of a-- of a pal, like you would say.
You know, visit each other from seeing--from going to-- Anyway, then I say, again to this railroad.
I went to the railroad, and I say, I have to go Warsaw, the capital.
I will get lost in the-- and I heard in the other Comitet, in the Polish Comitet with the refugees, they say, they have a very good arrangement in the capital, in Warsaw.
They do a lot of things for refugees, not like here.
They have the funds.
They have the volunteers.
After all, it's the capital, you know, and there are lots of aristocrats, the Polish living-- and I went to Warsaw.
I was riding, and they were checking out [NON-ENGLISH]-- permission to ride.
We went to a toilet.
Then anyway, when I was riding the-- I will never forget this-- there was sitting a young woman with me.
She got a little attache case.
And she was-- didn't look Jewish, naturally.
But she-- I have such-- I have such an instinct.
I don't know what the heck.
And we talked, and we talked.
And I thought, this young woman is running like I.
But she was by herself.
She was having this little attache probably lots and lots of money, which she did.
After this, we were sitting and talking, and I said listen, you know, I want to ask you something.
Don't hesitate.
You are Jewish, aren't you?
Oh, who told you?
And I said, I am, too.
We were having a secret together.
We talked and talked-- I even-- oh, look at your little son.
And I said, we are Jewish.
I'm telling you.
And when we find out about ourselves, and she also has a false paper.
And she also was-- in her case was different.
They came when they were arresting families-- different city, Lutsk.
It's near Rovno, another city.
And she ran.
Anyway, she was attractive, and she was my age, maybe younger, maybe older.
I was only 22 or 23 years old.
And then we were-- and we lost each other.
We arrived.
A conductor came in-- listen-- and we went, all three of us, went to the toilet sitting.
You've got to have-- it was war, and it was a risk-- you have to have permission to ride where, when.
Are you working with the Germans?
Whatever.
With the army, you-- you're working for them?
And we went to the toilet, all three of us.
We were sitting around.
I'll never forget.
She was talking, showing this hand holding the mouth towards [INAUDIBLE], shh, shh.
And we missed this visit.
We missed it.
Thank God, we walked out of the toilet, and we arrived in the station in Warsaw.
When we were riding the thing-- you see, listen.
I was there, and you would see me-- the way I was dressed, I looked like 40 years--
Oh, I'm sorry.
No, somebody has to-- no, no, it's not me.
I'm sorry.
You know why?
Somebody has to deliver Ensure.
Ensure.
They called me up, and they said they want me to gain weight.
And this is from the Cancer Society.
I'm sorry, I thought this is them.
[SIDE CONVERSATION] OK.
Then we are-- then a conductor, a Polish one, worked also in the-- in the railroad.
He came in.
He started a conversation with this nice girl.
You would see me, the way I looked-- I looked 40 years, not 22, with-- like a slob, like a homeless, which was true.
And she looked nice, attractive.
He started to have conversation with her.
He asked her where she's going when they arrive in Warsaw.
Where are we going to go?
She said she doesn't have-- she has a friend.
She going to find out about her boyfriend-- then OK.
Then he took her to her.
When we arrived in the station, he took her to him.
Who was going to take me?
I went there, again, the same story.
I was-- OK, then I went.
In the railroad there was a Polish officers working for the railroad.
They asked-- I told them I am a refugee, and I have to go to the office, but I don't have the-- the change, and I don't have the address, and I don't have the-- the time.
I remember they asked me to sit there.
They brought in something, a piece of bread with something-- with, I don't remember-- a piece of fruit.
And later, they took me-- gave me some change, and I went to the Polish Comitet.
It was different there.
It was-- I register--
I register.
I gave him my document.
I register.
They took a-- they took a-- what-- again, I asked already.
Where the nuns live?
Oh, the convent.
A convent.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I remember even the address.
The convent was for the disposal for the refugees-- several rooms, two floors.
Back in the other-- the other part of the building were for the nuns.
And there were altars-- you know, altars?
What do you call these things in church where you pray?
Where he preaches?
The altar.
It was, in Polish, oltarz.
There was the altars there, and they were always crossing themselves.
The refugees walking by, walking by.
They didn't remove it.
They are all Catholics.
And they gave us food, and it was all of this.
Somehow, somewhere, it was a good thing, for me at least-- solution.
Let me pray to God, always asking how is the front?
How is it going forward, the Germans-- when maybe-- Then after this thing, they separate us and they send us to a-- a different place with bunks, beds, one after the other.
You know bunks?
And then, while there, I met two girls.
I met two girls-- Sophia and Franka.
Francis and Sophia.
It became, frankly, all three of us.
They liked, very much, my son, and she's got-- one of them has also got a little boy.
He was younger than mine.
We decided we'll take a little place in the-- in the village, and we'll start it out of work.
I have to hide, and I was terrified for them.
They shouldn't find out, but I feel with them it's a little more-- I get lost in the crowd.
Well, we were stayed in this little place.
We rented it for a few dollars.
It's like a storage, but it was kind of nice in the village.
And we started-- they asked me for to be a translator.
I told them my parents, they came-- the reason why I know German is because one of my parents was from German background.
And I was trying not to talk too much, and they were crazy about this little boy, and I was always asking-- going after him.
One of them suspected-- or, she didn't suspect it, but we were-- we were there until they started to bomb this place.
And we were running away, and I walked away.
And I was running to the-- They were going to move out the Germans, further and further and further when they bombed it.
And we took-- they took us into our car, and will take us back father, farther, far away from the front.
I looked Francis and Sophia.
They were behind me in another car, and I don't see them.
And they said, don't worry, she's-- she's going to be arranged, also.
I lost them.
That's the end of this.
They took me to a place called Sucha where it was-- how can I tell you?
Farmers, the very rich farmers, is-- this is a title for a family like this.
Very rich farmers.
Is there a title for that?
The owners of lots of fields, lots of ground?
I don't know, but--
Very rich farm.
It was very rich farm, and they don't even have to do the farms.
They had people working for them.
You know, they go around, and they took trips.
This is a big farm, where the farmers were already evacuated, and the Germans took it over, naturally-- the units, the German units.
They gave us a place, gave me a place, to sleep with an elderly lady.
She worked in the kitchen.
A little bungalow.
And I worked in the kitchen also.
And we became friendly, and I just function.
I just function.
Let me just go and spend the time.
Let the time go by, by.
I became friendly, very much, with a German.
His name is Franz Knoler.
His name was Franz.
His last name was Knoler.
OK, I didn't tell you this story, now, again.
Now, I have to go back to this other hell.
When we ran away from-- when we ran away-- when I run away from Lemberg-- when I was in the field-- yes, yes, yes-- OK, OK, this is something different.

I started to work in soldatenheim-- called this is, what do you call for the Germans, for the-- not for the-- for the soldiers, when they go to the-- American soldiers, where they stop, and they eat, and they.
No.
They are special for army-- where even the volunteer movie stars work.
USO you call it?
Something you see in the television where they get served, and music, and things.
This was a soldatenheim.
Canteen-- canteen, I would-- this the better expression.
This was a canteen.
They called it soldatenheim.
It was a canteen where the were having shows, playing good music, and getting served.
And I worked there as a waitress, and then in the kitchen.
I always thinking about my little son.
He shouldn't be de-masked.
Once, I was going back from work.
And I was washing my feet.
I was washing-- there was sand, and I removed the old sandals.
I got some old, jalopy sandals.
I washed my feet near the pump.
You know a pump where it pumps water?
Primitive way?
And somebody grabs me on my waist, grabs my two shoulders, grabs me.
And they schlepped me away, and one of them raped me, and the other one wanted-- was going to rape me.
I saw from far away a little wagon with a horse.
I started to scream.
A little-- a horse with a wagon, a Polish peasant.
Started to scream.
They both of them disappeared.
I would never believe.
I was crying, screaming.
I came to the pump.
I washed myself.
I never believed that from a rape you can get pregnant.
I didn't know that.
People said you can't, and I was--I became pregnant.
When I was working in this canteen, there was this-- the Germans were very nice to me.
They were giving-- chocolate stuff to Zbyshek.
Once, I went to a doctor.
I didn't have my period.
I went to a doctor in the village, which was three miles, three kilometer, and he told me I'm pregnant.
And I said, sir, I couldn't be.
And I started to cry-- no, please, don't say that.
He said, ma'am, you are pregnant, and I don't remember how far.
And I told him the story.
And I says, well that's it.
This German Franz, also blond and blue eyed from Munich.
Would you believe it?
I met him in Munich in 1975?
Would you believe that?
And then I said-- then it was a woman, Barbara was her name.
Also, we became friends.
Of course, everything as a Catholic.
Everything as a Catholic.
And Barbara said, Maria, you are going to have to get rid of it.
I go to this doctor.
I'll take you.
I'll go with you, but you have to have-- I don't know, cigarettes, and wine.
They gave me, from the canteen, lots of these things, and I can give it to this man because the monies-- there was no even think about it.
I went there, and then I said, how am I going to go there?
How  I was going to take me back?
I'm going to need transportation.
And I told this secret to this Franz Knoler.
I started to cry, and he was very fond of me.
And he liked this little boy.
He was engaged to a girl-- he showed me the pictures in Munich.
And he was such a decent young man.
And he was also against the Nazis, anti-Nazi.
But he wouldn't--and I said, Franz, I'm-- and I started  to cry.
And he said, what happened?
And I said, I'm going to tell you.
And I told him the story.
And he said, Oh my god.
You don't know who he was?
I would kill him and this.
He said, I'll take you there, to this doctor.
Barbara and him, they took me to this doctor.
You've got the abortion.
He made the abortion.
I gave him the packages of cigarettes and wine, and he gave him something, this Franz.
When I came back, the abortion wasn't so very good, perhaps.
I bleeded and without stopping.
The lady, the old lady-- I  shared the bungalow-- the one who worked int he kitchen.
She said, Maria, there is something wrong here.
I went back to the doctor.
He took me again with this-- with this transportation and he said, In a minute everything is going to be all right.
Well, and since then I never could get pregnant.
That's what I want to tell you.
He ruined something there inside me.
I was raped.
I came back, and I was rid of it.
Oh, my God-- God-- and then, anyway--
I was holding on in this bungalow.
One of the Germans want to send me to a special kitchen, very hard work.
And they could see this Franz Knoler is very nice to me.
They sent him away in a different unit.
The officer said, this is a Polish woman.
Why do you so-- and the Polish baby.
Why do you carry on?
They transferred him in a different unit.
He came once, in the middle of the night, with a motorcycle-- would you believe it?
Asking me how I feel, and brought me some bread.
Well, that's another story.
Then I-- I met Germans-- said, you are going to have to work.
And we need additional hours in the night.
We'll wake you up.
Never mind the baby.
We have German women in our country.
We work nights.
It's a war.
And he started to pick on me.
And he suspected I'm Jewish.
And he said-- I'll never forget this-- girls, look what he made a gesture.
Look, Joan-- that's the gesture he made.
You're going to be locked up.
In these two hands he made the gesture.
I can't forget it.
You're going to be locked up.
Oh, you're going to be first in jail, makes a gesture like I'm going to be cross-crossed in a jail.
And I said to myself, OK, it's short time, and it's maybe the end-- maybe it's not, because you keep listening to the news.
They are getting farther and father.
They-- now with America joining in, and England, and France.
You know these stories.
And I, please I want to survive.
And finally, I left this place, and I went to another place.
The [NON-ENGLISH] in the streets-- the [NON-ENGLISH], megaphones, something, keep telling this front is coming down, down.
And finally, the Russians, the paratroopers came in.
And I couldn't believe it, God.
And I do speak Russian.
I just couldn't believe it.
I just wanted to jump.

I want to say, when I was in Warsaw-- going back for a minute-- I was riding this tram when was in  Warsaw, going to work, functioning, and we ride down the ghetto-- it was shortly after the end of the Warsaw Ghetto.
When I looked through there, and I see the windows, you can see because it's a very high parka-- a-- a wall, a wall.
But when you are high, you can see the windows open from the buildings.
And I could see a crib.
I could see hanging old pieces for curtains.
I used to ride it and say to myself, God, look at this places.
There were people, my people, Jewish people, children, going down with their life.
Look who they are.
And the Auschwitz burnt out.
Oy, God.
Anyway, then, finally, they ended.
The Russians marched in.
And would you believe something when I tell you?
Unbelievable, but It's true.
Would you believe the Russians arrested me?
Sit down, lady.
What are you being Jewish?
And what did you do with the Germans in a German unit and things?
Oy.
I said, I am Jewish, and my child is Jewish, and I have a false paper, and I was hiding.
Anyway, they let me go.
I am not crazy-- I wasn't crazy about the regime anyway, the communist regime, which is one big terrorist also.
It's another story.
Because like I told you, they marched in in '39 till '41.
Now, I survived.
I went to the-- to the-- to-- I went to the train.
I wanted to go to Rovno back.
Ask me what the hell for, I'll never know.
My life would change-- none of these is survived.
Nobody survived.
Anyway, Rovno is, again, not my-- oy.
I didn't know nothing, but my husband-- but somebody, when we were in  ghetto, somebody came and said, oh, Chiam Stolar, they caught him in the army, and he was killed.
And we were so sure he was killed, my husband.
I went to a city.
After they marched in, I went to another city, Lublin, which is near-- farther to Rovno.
And over there I registered and sleeped there with the refugees again and again.
I went to Rovno.
I went to Rovno with a terrible trip, and terrible, terrible, hell of a trip with the Russian soldiers.
They let us off, and no food.
This was another element, which wasn't far away from the Germans-- could tell you, whoever knows this communist, terrorist regime.
And I arrived in Rovno.
I didn't have a place to go.
Where should I go?
I went to Kola and Lucia-- they lived together in another apartment at the time being.
They were shocked and surprised that I am survived with Zbyshek.
She--there's another baby.
When I left, she was pregnant.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] husband, he was working with the underground, patriotic groups.
You know, the Polish underground?
And he was arrested and killed, and she was pregnant with another baby.
And she has already two-- one is my son's age, the other one  is younger.
I stayed with them.
I didn't have a place to go, and that was the end of the thing.
Should I continue?
Should I continue?
Sure.
That was that.
I stayed there and stayed with this animal.
Imagine staying with this animal.
I have no choice.
And he was, again, the same.
And people kept asking me, the war ended, why did you stay there?
Where should I go?
Where?
Oh, when I arrived-- first of all, when I arrived in Rovno, I didn't recognize the railroad station.
I knew the railroad station very well.
There was one big [NON-ENGLISH], one place.
It was everything bombed.
I wasn't there, in the meantime, and I wasn't there.
It was very high bombing coming in.
It was primitive little, made up a bungalow-- a primitive just to serve the passengers.
And I left my son there.
And I left him there with, I don't remember, with people from the railroad, and telling them I will be back.
And I went to Lucia and to Kola, and I find them that she left.
I told her-- she said, where's Zbyshek?
And I told her he's on the station.
She went with me.
We took a little wagon, and we took him back, first of all.
Then I stayed with them.
There was very terrible apartments, tiny apartment.
It goes on during [NON-ENGLISH], along with this [INAUDIBLE] with him, and he still continued to go to the bar.
He still continue to come back and he bashed, very much, his sister, too.
And I must say, I was raped from him again, because I sleep together with him-- there was no choice.
And on one-- in a nice day I came back, but then Tsila, my son's cousin, the girl who survived who's in Israel, not Fela, the one who brought him from the street.
My husband's sister's girl, she survived by the non-- non-Jewish brother-in-law hid them.
David was-- oh, I didn't mention the story with David.
Oh, my God.
David was killed.
He was hidden by a Polish family.
They [NON-ENGLISH] him, and they gave him away.
They were paid to hide him, and they gave him out.
David was killed.
Then Tsila, the younger sister, survived.
She was in a village in a Czech-- The non-Jewish brother-in-law have not any children, and they liked her.
He liked the sisters-- his wife's sister's little girl.
Then Tsila's a wife.
She's  in a Kibbutz in Israel, my son's first cousin.
Kristal.
No, you name after your husband.
She's a mother, of course.
Kristal.
It was Pomeranz, when she was young-- Pomeranz.
This non-Jewish brother-- brother of mine took to Tsila in.
Everybody was killed.
They discover, they find, Tsila's mother was hiding with a Polack-- the Polack was in love with her, kept her, and later he hadn't got a choice.
He left her, and she was killed.
The non-Jewish-- the non-Jewish brother's wife was killed.
She was-- she was walking around with a patch over one eye, you'll remember.
I was-- I was visiting-- I was sleeping a few nights there, a few nights there.
When-- when this-- when Kola went to get rid of me, I slept in a friend's house, a girl, and she said, don't go out.
Don't go out, Mary.
I will not, I said.
And she find out and she visited me-- the non-Jewish brother's-- brother's wife, my husband's sister.
She part-- and I said, Sonja, oh, my God-- why did you do it?
Why did you walk around?
I came from the village.
I came to straighten out.
And I says, why do you straighten out, when you have a village, you have a place for to stay?
Stay.
She's said, the placement there it's not bad, but she's with Tsila there.
The she's come in.
She-- oh, this is a very important part.
She made a patch on her eye.
She walked in, and we talked, and we talked.
And suddenly, somebody knocked on the door.
Go into door two men, Polacks.
He said to her, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Zbyshek was a little bit on the street for a change, this was when I stayed with my girlfriend for-- kind of a girlfriend-- for two days.
And he said, documents.
Where is your documents?
She said, oh, I don't have any.
Come.
He took her with the patch on her eye-- finished.
And there was standing here, this way, he was standing at the door.
One was standing near the door.
He was inside, asking the documents.
And I ran out through his arm.
I ran out through his arm, like he was standing in the door-- I ran out through his arm.
I said, oh, my God.
I grabbed my son, and I ran away.
If they find her, they probably you know about me, and I ran away.
Well, anyway, I ran away.
And going to go back to this man who arrested this Sonja, and this man who was standing at the door-- they were working with the Gestapo.
And after the war, when I was living in a city, I saw him.
I was walking with Lucia.
This is something has nothing to do with all of that.
I saw them walking, and we went to a policemen, a Polish policeman.
They arrested him.
Probably, they-- probably shot him.
He was a traitor, and giving out people for and dead.
Now, OK.
That's the story.
When I came back to Rovno.
I stayed with Lucia and Kola-- I hadn't got a place.
Tsila should have asked me.
OK.
